coredata.dc:title: Superior macro-scale tribological performance of steel contacts based on graphene quantum dots in aqueous glycerol

coredata.dc:description: Reducing friction is one of the best ways to lower energy consumption and make processes more environmentally friendly. Because of the growing interest in green lubricants, we investigated the synergetic effects of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) combined with aqueous glycerol to improve the lubrication performance of self-mated steel contacts in reciprocating sliding motion. As a comparison, the lubrication performance of some other two-dimensional (2D) graphitic materials (graphite, graphene oxide, and graphene nanoplatelets) was also studied. The results demonstrate that the GQDs-based nano-lubricant reduces the running-in period and provides super-low friction at a high contact pressure in the boundary-lubrication regime, with 72% and 53% improvements in anti-friction and anti-wear performance compared with aqueous glycerol. On the other hand, the 2D graphitic materials provide super-low friction in the mixed-lubrication regime due to the dominant polishing effect of the hydroxyl groups. The surface Raman mapping indicates that the 2D graphitic materials were severely damaged by the continuous reciprocating motion owing to their inferior crack resistance. In contrast, the superior deformation resistance of the GQDs helps to develop a tough tribofilm. This lubrication mechanism suggests that internal shearing of graphene layers inside the GQDs significantly reduces the wear and friction during the running-in period, while the in-situ formation of strongly adhered tough tribofilm with more surface coverage contributed to the realization of super-low friction under a high contact pressure. This study demonstrated that GQDs-based green nano-lubricants could provide super-low friction at a high contact pressure in the boundary-lubrication regime, which was so far characteristic mainly for conventional environment-polluting lubricants.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the methodology used to prepare the nano-lubricants.

Microstructural characterization of GQDs. (a) TEM image of GQDs (inset is a HRTEM image of GQDs with lattice spacings); (b) statistical lateral size distribution of GQDs determined by TEM; (c) XRD pattern of GQDs; (d) Raman spectra of GQDs, depicting strong D and G bands; (e) FTIR spectrum of GQDs; (f) high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of GQDs.

Long-term dispersion stability of graphitic materials in aqueous glycerol. (a) UV–vis spectra of Gr, GO, GNPs, and GQDs. (b) UV–vis spectra with 0.05, 0.075, and 0.1 wt% GQDs just after sonication (inset is a linear calibration curve of GQDs obtained from the absorbances at a wavelength of 560 nm). (c) Variation in UV–vis spectra of GQDs with time (inset the GQDsaq in daylight (left) and under 365 nm UV light(right)). (d) Change in relative concentration with time determining the dispersion stability of nano-lubricants with 0.1 wt% concentration measured by UV–vis spectrophotometry. (e) digital photographs of nano-lubricants with 0.1 wt% concentration taken 4 months after sonication.

Characterization of the tribological performance of graphitic materials (a) variation in COF in contacts with 0.1 wt% additive concentration as a function of sliding time (see Fig. S7 for 0.05 and 0.075 wt%); (b) Histogram of average COFs of contacts lubricated with nano-lubricants with different additive concentrations; (c) Wear-volume loss of the ball using nano-lubricants with different additive concentrations; (d) Optical photographs of ball’s wear scar lubricated with nano-lubricants with 0.1 wt% additive concentration; (e) Measured contact pressures (MCPs) for the aqueous glycerol and nanolubricants and their corresponding lubrication regimes; (f) Variation in the average COFs and MCP with different GQD concentrations.
SEM images of the wear scars on discs lubricated with nanolubricants with 0.1 wt% of the additive concentration.

Optical images and 2D Raman mappings of the worn surfaces and their corresponding Raman spectra. Raman mapping of characteristic G band on surfaces lubricated with (a) Graq, (b) GOaq, and (c) GNPsaq, respectively; (d) 2D Raman mapping for D/D+D′ characteristic band ratio on the surface lubricated with GQDsaq.

Change in the UV–vis spectrum of GQDs before and after friction test (inset Tauc plot of GQDs before and after friction test derived from UV–vis spectra). The inset shows photographs of the GQDs (a) in daylight, (b) under 365 nm UV light before friction test and (c) after friction test.
High-resolution XPS spectra of the elements: (a) C 1s, (b) O 1s on the worn surface of the disc.

Schematic of the mechanism for super-low friction in a self-mated steel contact lubricated with glycerol containing 2D graphitic materials. The oxidized surface is covered with FeOOH with OH termination ends interacting with the glycerol and graphene with a hydrogen bond.

Schematic of the proposed lubrication model for super-low friction at the self-mated steel interface. (a) schematic illustration indicating and formation of tough tribofilm; (b) schematic of the internal shearing effect of GQDs between asperities, (c) strongly adsorbed shear resistant layer of GQDs on the asperities, (d) adsorption mechanism of the GQDs on the positively charged worn surface, (e) OH-OH repulsion causing GQDs to slip past through asperities. Combination of all these effects facilitates the super-low friction at high contact pressure.
Peak positions and ID/IG ratios before and after friction tests. ratios before and after friction tests.

head.ce:title: Superior macro-scale tribological performance of steel contacts based on graphene quantum dots in aqueous glycerol

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reducing friction is one of the best ways to lower energy consumption and make processes more environmentally friendly. Because of the growing interest in green lubricants, we investigated the synergetic effects of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) combined with aqueous glycerol to improve the lubrication performance of self-mated steel contacts in reciprocating sliding motion. As a comparison, the lubrication performance of some other two-dimensional (2D) graphitic materials (graphite, graphene oxide, and graphene nanoplatelets) was also studied. The results demonstrate that the GQDs-based nano-lubricant reduces the running-in period and provides super-low friction at a high contact pressure in the boundary-lubrication regime, with 72% and 53% improvements in anti-friction and anti-wear performance compared with aqueous glycerol. On the other hand, the 2D graphitic materials provide super-low friction in the mixed-lubrication regime due to the dominant polishing effect of the hydroxyl groups. The surface Raman mapping indicates that the 2D graphitic materials were severely damaged by the continuous reciprocating motion owing to their inferior crack resistance. In contrast, the superior deformation resistance of the GQDs helps to develop a tough tribofilm. This lubrication mechanism suggests that internal shearing of graphene layers inside the GQDs significantly reduces the wear and friction during the running-in period, while the in-situ formation of strongly adhered tough tribofilm with more surface coverage contributed to the realization of super-low friction under a high contact pressure. This study demonstrated that GQDs-based green nano-lubricants could provide super-low friction at a high contact pressure in the boundary-lubrication regime, which was so far characteristic mainly for conventional environment-polluting lubricants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Friction and wear are two important factors in the failure of machinery and energy being wasted. Nearly 23% of global energy is consumed as frictional heat, ultimately leading to changes in the environment [1]. Thus, saving resources by reducing friction and wear is crucial to sustainable engineering, and an area where tribology and lubrication can make a real difference. In particular, greener contacts with greener tribology are becoming integral to new systems [2,3]. And in addition to biodegradable oils [4], polyhydric alcohols such as glycerol, myo-inositol, ethylene glycol (EG), 1,3-propylene glycol are being considered as water-soluble green lubricants [5]. Of these, glycerol is the most commonly used, exhibiting both cost-effectiveness and good biocompatibility [6]. Moreover, many tribological studies also reported ultra-low friction when using polyhydric alcohols [7–10].

Several researchers have used nanoparticles in polyhydric alcohols to achieve super-low friction [5,6,11–14]. Most of these studies report on uni-directional sliding motion [5,10,11,13,14]. However, despite the high initial contact pressure (i.e 0.7 – 1 GPa), the final contact pressure between the surfaces during super-low friction is very low, resulting from either tribochemical reactions or mechanical action during the running-in period. Chen et al. [5] achieved superlubricity in a mixed-mode lubrication regime between a self-mated steel contact with 0.01 wt% nanodiamonds (NDs) in aqueous glycerol. This reduction in friction is facilitated by the ball-bearing effect of nanodiamonds and a hydrogen-bond layer consisting of hydroxyl-terminated groups. Although NDs improve the wear rate compared to aqueous glycerol, the corresponding contact pressure during the superlubricity period was reduced to about 50 MPa, which was significantly lower than the initial contact pressure. Furthermore, the superlubricity was destroyed with an increase in the sliding speed. Therefore, achieving super-low friction on engineering steel surfaces under a high contact pressure remains a challenge.

Since the discovery of graphene, extensive research has been conducted on graphene nano-lubricants owing to their unique anti-wear and anti-friction properties. Their mechanical properties contribute to wear resistance; the thermal conductivity promotes heat dissipation during sliding; while the impermeability of the graphene protects the tribofilm from oxidation and corrosion [15]. Moreover, graphene is considered to be much greener than carbon nanotubes [16] and shows better frictional properties [6,12,13,17,18]. Recently, Esquivel-Gaon et al. [17] found that automotive lubricants based on reduced graphene oxide (GO) reduced the coefficient of friction in all lubrication regimes and reduced the environmental impact of transportation. Despite the excellent tribological properties and the green nature of graphene-based nano-lubricants, their use on an industrial scale is hampered by their tendency to agglomerate, the ability to sustain only a low contact pressure during super-low friction [12], the restricted interlayer slippage [15,19], the limited time of the superlubricity [20], and the instability between the micro-asperities, owing to their large size in a lateral direction [21].

With the advancements in nanotechnology and optoelectronics, in 2006 Sun et al. developed stable photoluminescent carbon dots [22]. Carbon-based quantum dots, particularly graphene quantum dots (GQDs) derived from GO, are considered a potential replacement for graphene and GO as an additive in liquid base lubricants, thanks to their outstanding physicochemical properties and zero-dimensional spherical nanostructure. Interestingly, GQDs are even less toxic than GO [23]. Unlike carbon dots, GQDs are considered as a smaller version of GO containing a graphene structure inside the dots, which is responsible for their excellent physical properties [24]. In 2019, Tang and co-workers [21] proposed that carbon-based quantum dots used as an additive in deionized water can reduce the coefficient of friction by 33% due to the nano-bearing and nano-filling effects of the carbon dots. Later, it was proposed that the formation of a protective tribofilm is the reason for the improved frictional and wear performance [25]. Currently, GQDs are seeing interest as additives for polyhydric alcohols. No studies (apart from one involving a Si3N4/sapphire tribopair [14]) have demonstrated that the addition of graphene-oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) in an EG aqueous solution reduces the wear by 90% and provides superlubricity by forming a tribofilm consisting of GOQDs.

In this study we investigated the macroscale lubrication performance of GQDs as an additive in aqueous glycerol between self-mated steel contacts. Further, we compared the frictional and anti-wear performance of different graphitic materials as an additive in aqueous glycerol in reciprocating sliding motion for the first time. Prior to our investigation, the lubrication performance of GQDs in aqueous glycerol was unknown for common industrial materials such as bearing steel, as this is a relatively new, but very promising, material for green tribology.

Commercially available pure glycerol derived from a renewable feedstock with more than 99.5% purity, synthetic graphite powder (Gr) with a platelet size less than 20 µm, and GO powder (15–20 sheets, 4–10% edge oxidized) were acquired from Sigma Aldrich. Graphene nanoplatelets (GNPs) were provided by Graphene Supermarket, comprising 3–8 graphene monolayers with a thickness of less than 3 nm. GQDs with red luminescence (and an average size of 10 nm) were organically synthesized and provided by Nanjing XFNANO Materials.

The microstructure and morphologies of the graphitic materials were determined using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM; JEOL, JEM2010). The crystal structure of the nanomaterials was evaluated using X-ray diffraction (XRD; D8/Advance, Bruker, Germany) operated at room temperature using Co–Kα radiation over a 2ϴ range of 5–45˚ with a step size of 0.02˚. The confocal Raman spectrophotometer (alpha 300 RS, Witec, Germany) was used at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm to identify the structural units of the nanomaterials. An attenuated-total-reflection Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (ATR-FTIR; Perkin Elmer instruments, USA) was used to investigate the surface functional groups, while the chemical structure of the GQDs was analyzed using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS; PHI-TFA, Physical Electronics Inc, USA). The zeta (ζ) potential (Zetasizer NanoZS, Malvern, United Kingdom) was used to measure the surface charge on the GQDs by preparing a 0.1-mg/mL solution in ethanol as a solvent.

The aqueous glycerol was prepared by mixing glycerol (90 wt%) with demi water (10 wt%), followed by stirring for 1 h to ensure homogenization. We used 10 wt% demi-water to lower it freezing point to − 1°C [26], subsequently this also drastically reduced the viscosity of the mixture (i.e., 131.5 mm2/s, which is almost 81.7% less compared to pure glycerol) to ensure the boundary lubrication conditions. Increasing the water content in glycerol promotes steel wear, which is not desirable [7]. Then graphitic materials (Gr, GO, GNPs and GQDs) were added to the aqueous glycerol in amounts of 0.05, 0.075, and 0.1 wt%, respectively. The concentrations were selected based on positive results from the literature [5,12,20]. The mixtures were sonicated for 45 min using a bath sonicator with a power input of 500 W (Sonis 4 GT - Iskra pio d.o.o., Šentjernej, Slovenia). Finally, the mixture was subjected to stirring using a magnetic hotplate (WiseStir MSH-20D, Germany) to ensure the homogenous dispersion of the graphitic materials in aqueous glycerol. Fig. 1 shows a detailed schematic of the preparation of the nano-lubricants, denoted Graq, GOaq, GNPsaq, and GQDsaq.

The sedimentation method is not considered suitable to evaluate the dispersion stability of carbonaceous materials due to their dark color and an inability to clearly distinguish the amount of sedimentation. Therefore, an ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (Hewlett-Packard-8453, Palo Alto, CA) was used to obtain the absorption spectra over a period of time to investigate the long-term dispersion stability of graphitic materials in aqueous glycerol. The dispersions were placed in quartz cuvettes with an optical path length of 1 cm and a scanning wavelength range of 200–800 nm. According to the Lambert-Beer Law, the concentration is proportional to the absorbance [27]. Linear calibration curves were obtained between the absorbance and the concentration immediately after the sonication for each nano-lubricant at specific wavelengths. Then absorption spectra of each nano-lubricant were recorded for up to 72 h over a wavelength ranging between 200 and 800 nm. The relative concentrations were plotted against time for each nano-lubricant to evaluate their dispersion stability.

The friction and wear tests were conducted in reciprocating motion using a ball-on-flat-disc geometry and a universal micro-tribotester (UMT-2, Bruker, USA). Contact pairs, i.e., disc and balls with 10 mm diameter, were made of AISI 52100 steel and prepared with multi-axis grinding and subsequent polishing to a surface roughness (Rq) of 0.03 µm. Prior to the testing, the balls and discs were ultrasonically cleaned with heptane and acetone. Before the start of each test, a 50-µL solution was carefully placed between the ball and the disc, as illustrated in Fig. 2. The applied load was 3.4 N, corresponding to a maximum Hertzian contact pressure of 700 MPa. Each test lasted 75 min at a frequency of 10 Hz and a stroke length of 5 mm, corresponding to a sliding speed of 0.1 m/s. These contact conditions were selected to ensure boundary lubrication (see Supplement material, Note S1). All the tests were conducted under ambient conditions (25 °C, 30–35% RH) and repeated three or four times to ensure repeatability. Fig. 2 shows a schematic of the test configuration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wear determination and characterization of the worn surface’s topography
ce:section.ce:para: After the tribological tests the worn surfaces of the balls and discs were thoroughly cleaned using isopropanol, and their morphologies, surface roughnesses and wear volumes were characterized using an optical microscope (Nikon - LV150) and a 3D white-light interferometer (Bruker – Contour GT-K0).

In addition, the topography of the worn surface was examined using a scanning electron microscope (SEM; JEOL, JSM IT100) coupled with a dry Si-drift detector operated at an acceleration voltage of 20 kV. The worn surfaces were analyzed with 2D Raman mapping, while the chemical nature of the worn surfaces lubricated with GQDsaq was investigated with XPS. The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained after the tribological test to detect the changes in the structure of the graphitic materials. For this purpose, a 200 µL solution was carefully placed between the tribo-pairs, and friction tests were performed with the same parameters. At the end of each test, 50 µL of the solution was carefully collected from the sample surface using a pipette and diluted with aqueous glycerol at a ratio of 1:29. Similarly, unused nanolubricants were diluted with aqueous glycerol and measured using the UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The microstructural features of the nanomaterials are important for tribological performance. Fig. 3a shows that the GQDs are monodisperse in size with an average particle size of 8.1 ± 1.4 nm, as indicated in Fig. 3b. The HRTEM image (inset Fig. 3a) shows the clear lattice fringes of the GQDs with a lattice spacing of 0.33 nm, which corresponds to the (002) diffraction plane of the graphite. The AFM analysis (Fig. S1) revealed that the topographic height of GQDs ranges from 2 to 4.5 nm, suggesting that GQDs are anisotropic with height smaller than their lateral dimensions, as also reported by Zhu et al. [28]. Similarly, the microstructures and morphologies of the Gr, GO, and GNPs are shown in Fig. S2 (see supplement material, Note S2). The XRD further confirmed the crystalline nature of the graphitic materials. Unlike the Gr, GO, and GNPs (Fig. S3a), the XRD spectrum of the GQDs shows a characteristic peak at 31.8˚, which is a higher 2ϴ value than for other graphitic materials (Fig. 3c), representing a lattice spacing of 0.33 nm, which is consistent with the TEM observations (Fig. 3a). This contraction in the interlayer spacing is due to the π-π stacking of small graphene layers with few structural defects or possible hydrogen bonding between the oxygen-containing functional groups surrounding the edges of the GQDs [29]. Fig. 3a and c further suggest that the GQDs have a nanocrystalline core of sp2 graphitic carbon atoms.

Raman spectroscopy is a non-destructive way of investigating the structural changes in nanoscale carbon [30]. The Raman spectrum of GQDs showed characteristic D and G bands at about 1354 and 1554 cm−1, respectively, with an ID/IG ratio of 0.97 (Fig. 3d). This increase in the D band’s intensity and the shifting of the G band to a lower wavenumber indicated the presence of an excess quantity of functional groups and a high edge-to-volume ratio. Lin et al. [31] suggested that this downshifting of the G peak is due to the electron-donating capability of nitrogen-heteroatoms. The GQDs had no higher-order 2D band, indicating that they have a multilayered structure that leads to good lubrication [32]. Similarly, Fig. S3b shows the Raman spectra of the Gr, GO, and GNPs (see Supplement material, Note S2).

The ATR-FTIR spectrum of the GQDs further confirms the existence of excess functional groups (Fig. 3e). The ATR-FTIR spectra show N-H and O-H stretching vibrations in the range 3000–3500 cm−1, while the C-H stretching vibrations are between 2750 and 2985 cm−1. The peaks at 1624 and 1654 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CO and CC [33], while the stretching-vibration peaks at 1492, 1330, and 1093 cm−1 demonstrate the presence of C-H, C-OH, and C-O, respectively. The peaks at 1275 and 1195 cm−1 can be attributed to the C-N/N-H and C-O/C-N stretching vibrations, respectively [34,35].

XPS analyses revealed more information about the functional groups and the interlinkages within the GQDs. The XPS survey spectra of the GQDs (Fig. S4a) revealed that the atomic percentages of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen were 62.5, 20.7, and 16.8 at%, respectively. Furthermore, the N/C ratio was 0.27, which is identical to the value reported by Gómez et al. [36], indicating appropriate nitrogen doping in the GQDs. The presence of nitrogen-containing bonds and functional groups is due to the solution chemistry approach used for synthesizing GQDs. These approaches typically use citric acid as the carbon source and various amines (e.g., urea, melamine, etc.) as the nitrogen source [37]. A high-resolution C 1s spectrum of the GQDs (Fig. 3f) was deconvoluted into five components, corresponding to CC/C–C (284.7 eV), C–OH/C–N (285.8 eV), CO/CN (287.4 eV), O–CO (288.7 eV), and a π-π * satellite group (290.5 eV) [35,36]. In addition, the N 1s spectrum (Fig. S4b) shows typical peaks at 399.9 and 402 eV, corresponding to the C-N/NH2 [36] and N-H bonds[38], respectively. The XPS results indicate that the surface components identified by the XPS are in good agreement with the FTIR results.

UV–vis absorption was used to characterize the graphitic materials and their dispersion stability in aqueous glycerol ( Fig. 4). As shown in Fig. 4a, in the case of the Gr, a shoulder was detected at a wavelength of ∼272 nm, with no other distinct peak being observed [39]. In contrast, the GNPs and GO showed strong absorption peaks at 271 and 263 nm, attributable to the π-π * transition of the aromatic C–C bond (Fig. 4a). However, unlike the absorption peak of the GO at around ∼237 nm [40], the peak was redshifted to 263 nm, which confirms the 4–10% edge oxidation of the GO [41]. This result agrees well with the TEM, XRD, and Raman observations (see supplement material, Note S2). Also, the shoulder at ∼300 nm was observed for both the GO and GNPs, corresponding to the n-π * transitions of the conjugated CO bonds. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the GQDs shows a shoulder at ∼245 nm and absorption peaks at 365 and 560 nm (Fig. 4a and b). The shoulder at ∼245 nm represents the π-π * transition of the sp2 aromatic domains in the sp3 carbon matrix. In contrast, the absorption peaks at 365 nm represent the n-π * transitions of the conjugated CO and CN bonds in an sp2 aromatic system [42], whereas the absorption peak at 560 nm corresponds to the n-π * transition of the free electrons of the adatoms (e.g., C–O, C–NH2, etc.) [43]. These adatoms facilitate the absorption by altering the surface structure or generating additional edge sites [43]. The inset of Fig. 4c shows that the GQDs emit pink light when being irradiated with UV light of 365 nm.

Fig. 4b illustrates the absorption spectrum of different concentrations of GQDs in aqueous glycerol immediately after sonication. It is clear that the absorption increased with the concentration of GQDs, as suggested by the Lambert-Beer law [27]. Similar behavior was observed for the Gr, GO, and GNPs (Fig. S5). The inset of Fig. 4b shows a linear calibration curve for GQDs. The curve was obtained by measuring the absorbance values at a wavelength of 560 nm. Similarly, linear calibration curves were plotted for the Gr at 367 nm, while absorbance at 660 nm was chosen for the GO and GNPs to investigate the dispersion stability versus storage time (Fig. S5) [44]. Fig. 4c shows that the absorbance value was almost the same, with no significant loss in absorbance of the GQDs being observed even after 72 h, indicating an excellent dispersion stability. As shown in Fig. S5c, a considerable loss in absorbance was observed for the Gr dispersion, while no substantial loss of absorbance was observed for the GO and GNPs containing aqueous glycerol. Fig. 4d shows the relative concentration of 0.1 wt% graphitic materials in aqueous glycerol over a period of time. This is maintained at over 95% for the GO, GNPs, and GQDs. However, the Gr showed a 15% reduction in relative concentration after just 4 h of sonication, indicating a poor dispersion stability. The relative concentrations of the 0.05 and 0.075 wt% graphitic materials in aqueous glycerol with sedimentation time showed similar behavior (Fig. S6). The digital photographs of the 0.1 wt% dispersions shown in Fig. 4e reveal that the GNPs-, GO-, and GQDs-based nano-lubricants retained their homogeneity even 4 months after their preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the type of graphitic material and its concentration on the tribological performance
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of different graphitic materials (Gr, GO, GNPs, GQDs) and aqueous glycerol on the reduction in friction is shown in Fig. 5. The baseline experiment conducted using aqueous glycerol showed that the steady-state coefficient of friction (COF) lies in the range 0.09–0.11. The observed high COF indicates the inability of aqueous glycerol to form an effective lubricating film. On the other hand, adding a small amount of graphitic material significantly reduced the COF compared with the aqueous glycerol (Fig. 5a and b). In the case of Graq, the lowest steady-state COFs, i.e., ∼0.030–0.032, were observed at concentrations of 0.05 wt% and 0.075 wt%; however, a long running-in time of around 25 min could lead to high wear of the tribo-pairs. As the Gr concentration increased to 0.1 wt%, the COF increased to 0.038, but this was still 61% less than that of the aqueous glycerol (Fig. 5b). In the case of the GOaq, the steady-state COFs at 0.05 wt% and 0.075 wt% lie in the range 0.026–0.027. With an increase in the GO concentration to 0.1 wt%, the COF becomes unstable and increases to 0.052, which is more than twice as much as with the GQDs (Fig. 5a). It is evident that the running-in time was reduced to 5–7 min if the GO were added to the aqueous glycerol. The addition of the GNPs to the aqueous glycerol significantly reduced the COFs, which were 26% and 18% less than those of the Gr and GO, respectively. It should be noted that the running-in time was shortened twofold with an increase in the concentration of the GNPs from 0.05 to 0.1 wt%, but it was still more than 25 min (Fig. S7). In contrast, compared with the GNPsaq, the GQDsaq shortened the running-in by 4.5, 2.5, and 2 times with concentrations of 0.05, 0.075, and 0.1 wt%, respectively (Fig. 5a and S7). With 0.1 wt% GQDs, the COF reduced to ∼0.020 after 12 min of the running-in period, and reached the stable final value of ∼0.025 by the end of the test, which is a 72% improvement over the aqueous glycerol. An additional test performed with 0.1 wt% GQDs over a longer period also confirm that the steady-state COF lies in the range of 0.024–0.025 (Fig. S8). This super-low friction is suggested to be due to the formation of a lubrication film and is discussed later.

The anti-wear performance of the disc lubricated with three different concentrations (0.05 wt%, 0.075 wt% and 0.1 wt%) of graphitic material is shown in Fig. 5c. Adding graphitic material to the aqueous glycerol greatly improved the wear performance compared to the aqueous glycerol. The addition of the Gr reduced the wear compared with aqueous glycerol; however, with an increase in the concentration to 0.1 wt%, the wear volume increased by 54%. The GO and GNPs showed a similar trend, i.e., the wear volumes increased by 41% and 38%, respectively, when the concentration was increased from 0.05 to 0.1 wt%. In contrast, the wear volume of the GQDs decreased slightly with an increase in the concentration. Overall, the addition of 0.1 wt% GQDs reduced the wear by more than 2 times compared with 0.05 wt% GQDsaq. This behavior is also evident in Fig. 5d. The wear scar on the ball lubricated with GQDsaq was much smaller than other competing materials. The WSD of the ball lubricated with 0.1 wt% GQDsaq was about 148.5 µm, smaller than those WSDs lubricated with aqueous glycerol (362 µm) (see Fig. S10b), Graq (198.7 µm), GOaq (228.7 µm), and GNPsaq (200.9 µm), respectively (Fig. 5d). The 3D surface topography and the wear profile of the ball wear scars after friction tests are presented in Fig. S9. Similarly, the WSDs of the ball lubricated with the nanolubricants having 0.05 and 0.075 wt% additive concentration are presented in Table S1 and Fig. S11, which further validate the data shown in Fig. 5c. It is known that the WSD negatively correlates with the contact pressure and positively correlates with the contact area. The contact pressure in the super-low-friction period was estimated by dividing the normal load (3.4 N) by the contact area (worn area on the ball). Moreover, it was found that λ > 2 in the case of the Graq, GOaq, and GNPsaq, suggesting that the super-low friction is in the mixed-lubrication regime (see supplement material, Note S6), while the corresponding contact pressures during the super-low friction are much lower compared with the GQDs (Fig. 5e). In contrast, the λ is around 0.51 for the GQDsaq (see supplement material, Note S6), suggesting that super-low friction is observed in the boundary-lubrication regime at higher contact pressures (Fig. 5e).

Fig. 5f shows the influence of the GQDs’ concentration on the contact pressure and the average COF during super-low friction. The average COF of the aqueous glycerol decreased from 0.09 to 0.029 with the addition of 0.05 wt% GQDs, after which it decreased further to 0.024 and 0.025 with an increase in the concentration to 0.075 and 0.1 wt%, respectively. On the other hand, by adding 0.05 wt% GQDs, the contact pressure during the super-low friction period increased from 36.76 MPa to 134.70 MPa, which further increased to 206.20 MPa when increasing the concentration to 0.1 wt%, with an increment of 460.9% compared with the aqueous glycerol.

Fig. 6 shows an SEM photograph of the surface lubricated with 0.1 wt% concentration of all the nano-lubricants. As is clear from Fig. S10, the surface lubricated with aqueous glycerol had deep grooves and pits inside the worn surface. In addition, the maximum wear depth was around 300 nm, indicating severe abrasive wear caused by the material removal and plastic deformation (Fig. S10). Overall, the wear tracks of the surface lubricated with graphitic materials were relatively smooth, indicating no significant wear compared with the aqueous glycerol. The SEM image of the surfaces lubricated with 0.1 wt% of Graq, GOaq, and GNPsaq revealed that the surfaces were smooth, and at the same time there were some pits and local ploughing features (Fig. 6a). While the worn surface with 0.1 wt% GOaq lubricant showed prominent polishing and some pits on the surface, there was no evidence of plastic deformation (Fig. 6b). Unlike the GOaq and Graq, the worn surface of the 0.1 wt% GNPsaq showed a slight polishing effect with very little evidence of wear as the surface-preparation scratches can be seen on the surface (Fig. 6c). The surface lubricated with the 0.1 wt% GQDsaq revealed slight furrows, implying the physical interaction of the GQDs with the surface, which could be a reason for the reduction in the COF at a higher contact pressure. Also, surface-preparation scratches were visible, indicating superior anti-wear behavior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman analysis of structural changes in the wear track
ce:section.ce:para: Raman mapping inside the wear tracks was carried out to gain more insight into the active lubrication mechanism ( Fig. 7). Overall, the Raman spectra on the worn surfaces showed a graphitic structure with a stronger D band, while the intensity of the 2D band decreased and the G peak shifted towards a higher wavenumber, implying a significant increase in the ID/IG ratio (3–5 folds) for the surface lubricated with Graq, GOaq and GNPsaq ( Table 1). In contrast, there is a slight change in the ID/IG for the GQDsaq during the period of friction. Concurrently, the G peak is shifted towards a higher wavenumber, with a broadening of the D and G bands.

As shown in the 2D Raman mapping (Fig. 7), the areas with bright yellow and orange patches revealed the signature of the G band. In comparison, the area of wear in brown showed the absence of Raman active modes. The Raman mapping of the worn surface lubricated with Graq, indicated that the surface is not fully covered with graphite, and there is no continuous tribofilm on the surface (Fig. 7a). Raman spectra were obtained in three regions, clearly shown by the pixels of different colors. At points 2 and 3 there is a distinct D band at 1346.0 cm−1, while the intensity of the G band decreases, and shifts from 1578.3 to 1601.8 cm−1 after the friction test. At the same time, the intensity of the 2D peak decreases, and the D+D′ band becomes more prominent. The peak at around 3271 cm−1 (point 2) and 3268 cm−1 (point 3) corresponds to the HOH and OH bonds [45]. Point 3 is slightly different to point 2, as there is a weak peak at about 660 cm−1, which relates to FeOOH [46]. It is noteworthy (see point 3) that the D band is splitting, and the shoulder at around 1432 cm−1 relates to defect-induced broadening [47].

The Raman mapping of the distinct G band on the worn surface lubricated with GOaq revealed that the GO squeeze out from the sliding contact and the surface was not covered with GO, as demonstrated by the Raman spectra at point 1 (Fig. 7b). There is a visible difference in the Raman spectra at point 2 (at the edges of the wear track) and point 3 (inside the wear track). Inside the wear track (point 3) there are some additional peaks at 590 and 655 cm−1, corresponding to the formation of Fe2O3 and FeOOH [46]. The peaks at less than 300 cm−1 belong to iron oxides [46]. However, these peaks were not observable outside the wear track (point 2), probably due to the thick graphene layer at the edges of the wear tracks. At both points there was a significant drop in the intensity of the G band, while it was shifted towards a higher wavenumber (1573.6 to 1608.1cm−1). The intensity of the 2D band decreased substantially, indicating that the GO maintains its multilayer structure.

In the case of a surface lubricated with GNPsaq, the Raman mapping indicated that the graphene platelets partially filled the grooves on the surface. At the same time, there was no continuous tribofilm on the surface (point 1, Fig. 7c). The Raman spectra at point 2 revealed that the ID/IG ratio increased from 0.18 to 0.97, and along with peak broadening, the G band slightly shifted from 1577.5 to 1604.1 cm−1. Moreover, the 2D band at ∼2700 cm−1 was less prominent, and there was an additional D+D′ band at ∼2936 cm−1. The weak peaks between 3100 and 3400 cm−1 indicated the OH and HOH stretching bonds [48]. At point 3 (around the edges of the grooves), the Raman spectra only showed the presence of iron oxide and hydroxide, and there are no prominent D and G bands [46,49].

Interestingly, the Raman spectra on the surface lubricated with GQDsaq revealed the appearance of higher-order 2D and D+D′ bands after the period friction. Therefore, the Raman mapping for the D/D+D′ characteristic band ratio was used to distinguish the regions with and without higher-order bands, as shown in Fig. 7d. There was no significant variation in the ID/IG before and after the friction test (Table 1). However, the G band was broadened and shifted to a higher wavenumber, indicating an increase in the number of carbon atoms bonded to either an adjacent carbon or oxygen in the form of hydroxyl or epoxy groups by sp3 hybridization (Fig. 7d) [50]. Furthermore, the Raman mapping revealed that the GQDs tribofilm had more surface coverage than the worn surface lubricated with Graq, GOaq and GNPsaq (see Fig. 7a, b and c). The Raman spectra of the bright-yellow region (point 2) revealed the absence of a D+D′ band, meaning that the GQDs around the edges of the grooves have no ordering of graphene layer along the c-axis [51].

In contrast, the Raman spectra at points 3 and 4 revealed that the two new Raman peaks, i.e., the 2D peak centered at 2720 cm−1 and D+D′ band centered at 2986 cm−1, appeared, indicating the sliding-induced transformation of the GQDs inside the nano-valleys and grooves (Fig. 7d) [52]. Additional peaks were observed at around 515 and 614 cm−1, confirming the formation of Fe2O3 and FeOOH, respectively [49]. The peak at about 794 cm−1 corresponds to the C-C stretching of glycerol molecules and is very close to the values reported by Essendoubi et al. [53]. The peak and shoulder at about 1109 and 1260 cm−1 correspond to the C-OH/C-N stretching and C-H bending modes, respectively [45,54,55]. Then the peaks at ∼982.5 and 1485 cm−1 correspond to the CH2 vibrations of the glycerol molecules [45]. Finally, a small hump at about 3242 cm−1 corresponds to the O-H stretching mode [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes in surface properties of GQDs after friction test
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 shows the UV–vis spectrum of glycerol containing GQDs before and after the tribological test. There is a redshift in the high-energy absorption bands and a significant increase in the absorbance after the friction test. The redshifted shoulder and peak at ∼278 and ∼370 nm correspond to the π-π * and n-π * transition of CC and CO bonds, respectively, in an aromatic ring and do not usually contribute to the fluorescence. This redshift indicates the restoration of the π-conjugated network of graphene owing to the formation of new sp2-bonded atoms during the sliding [56]. Interestingly, a visible blue shift in the low-energy region of the UV–vis spectrum indicates the different surface and edge states of the GQDs before and after the friction test. Furthermore, after the friction test, the GQDs emitted a light-green color when irradiated with a UV light of 365 nm (see inset Fig. 8), indicating that the surface states dominate the photoluminescence behavior of the GQDs. Since iron nanoparticles or debris do not show an absorption band in the visible range [57], the band at about 550 nm is specifically from the GQDs. Therefore, this change in the surface electronic state is either due to excessive -OH groups from glycerol degradation during sliding [58] or the reduction of carbonyl or epoxy moieties on the GQDs to hydroxyl groups, causing an increase in the hydroxyl-terminated groups [58]. It is well known that the hydroxyl groups are electron-donating, and increasing their quantity can improve the luminescence of the GQDs [59]. The optical band gap estimated from the Tauc plot derived from the UV–vis absorption spectrum also reveals an increase in the bandgap from 1.95 to 2.0 eV due to an enriched electron density caused by the -OH groups (inset Fig. 8), also consistent with the color of their fluorescence [60]. Thus, a combination of the smaller sp2 domains and the more significant proportion of the hydroxyl functional groups is responsible for the blue-shifted emission. This finding further validates the shifting and broadening in the G band observed in the Raman spectra of the worn surfaces lubricated with GQDsaq (see Fig. 7d).

In the case of the Graq, after the tribological test, the absorption peak observed at ∼270 nm was redshifted to ∼282 nm, and the absorption intensity increased after the friction test, indicating more graphite sheets in the aqueous glycerol due to the severe exfoliation during the reciprocating sliding (Fig. S13a). Furthermore, this increase in absorption intensity suggests the restoration of CC bonds within the graphene, to some extent [56]. The UV–vis spectra of the GOaq and GNPsaq were similar before and after the tribological tests (Fig. S13b and c). Moreover, no visible shift in the absorption maxima before and after the friction test suggested no friction oxidation [6].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of chemical changes in the wear track lubricated with GQDsaq
ce:section.ce:para: The XPS of the worn surface revealed that the atomic percentages of carbon, oxygen, iron, and nitrogen are about 52.1 at%, 37.4 at%, 8.7 at%, and 1.8 at%, respectively. The C 1s XPS spectra on the worn surface indicated the presence of C–C/CC, C-OH/C-N, CO and O–CO groups at binding energies of 284.7 eV, 286.2 eV, 288.2 eV, and 289.2 eV, respectively ( Fig. 9a). Compared with the C 1s spectra of GQDs (Fig. 3f), the relative concentration of CO with respect to the total C signal reduced to 9%, while the relative concentration of the C–C/CC bonds increased from 41.7% to 69.2%. In contrast, the concentration of C-OH/C-N was 19.1%. Similarly, the O 1s spectrum revealed that the peak centered at a binding energy of 529.8 eV corresponds to iron oxide, while the peak at 531.2 eV was attributed to either iron hydroxide or OC [8,61] (Fig. 9b). Moreover, the peak at around 532.6 eV was attributed to C–OH bonds [62], while the peak centered at 533.3 eV corresponds to water molecules, either adsorbed from the humid air or produced from glycerol degradation. The corresponding low intensity of CO in the C 1s spectrum confirms the dominance of hydroxides or C–OH. The hydroxides and -OH groups played an important role in forming a hydrogen-bond network with excessive -OH terminated groups on the surface of the GQDs [5,8,11,13,58]. Furthermore, the Raman and UV–vis spectra findings after the friction test confirmed the abundance of hydroxyl groups attached to the GQDs.

The results indicate that the GQDs, GO, and GNPs exhibit excellent dispersion stabilities in aqueous glycerol (Fig. 4). This is due to the strong hydrogen-bonding interaction between the hydrophilic functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxy) on their surface and the glycerol (Fig. 3e). Furthermore, due to their small size, the high edge-to-volume ratio, the large surface area, and the abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups, the GQDs outperformed the other graphitic materials in terms of dispersion stability and can remain stable in aqueous solutions for up to 1 year [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of size, morphology and concentration on the friction and wear behavior
ce:section.ce:para: The key point of the friction results presented in Fig. 5 is that adding even a very small amount of graphitic material, smaller than in many similar studies [18,25], reduced the COF compared with the aqueous glycerol. The analyses of the worn surfaces indicated that the Graq, GOaq, and GNPsaq have similar lubrication behavior, although the lubrication mechanism for the GQDs is different. First of all, the GQDs have better anti-wear performance than the Gr, GO, and GNPs (Fig. 5c and d). This is because the size of the GQDs (Fig. 3a) is significantly smaller than the surface roughness (i.e., 30 nm). As a result, as indicated by the Raman mapping (Fig. 7d), they can easily fill in the valleys and irregularities on the surface and improve the lubrication by artificially smoothening the surface. Furthermore, due to their spherical morphology, the GQDs exhibit superior extreme-pressure and load-carrying characteristics owing to their inherent spherical-shape mechanics-support effect. In contrast, the Gr, GO, and GNPs cannot penetrate the nano-valleys due to their larger size. Likewise, it is clear from Fig. 7b that the GO squeezes out from the rubbing surfaces, forming an aggregate at the edges of the wear track. This causes the unstable friction behavior of GO, as seen in Fig. 5a.

As presented in Fig. 5b and c, the tribological behavior of the graphitic materials has a strong dependence on the concentration. In the case of the GQDs, the average COF and the wear volume decreased with an increase in the concentration (Fig. 5b and c). This improvement in tribological performance could be ascribed to mechanisms like tribo-layer formation, nano-bearing, and the self-healing effect in frictional interfaces [14,21,25]. According to Tang et al. [21], 0.1 wt% is the best concentration for better lubrication, and a further increase in the concentration could lead to the aggregation of dissolved carbon dots and so increase the friction.

For the Graq and GOaq it was observed that the average COF and wear volume increased with an increase in the concentration (Fig. 5b and c). This deterioration in the tribological properties could be due to the aggregation of graphene sheets, either with an increase in the concentration or severe damage, leading to the formation of micron- or sub-micron-size sediments. In the case of the GNPs, the average COF decreases with increasing concentration, while the wear volume does not change significantly (Fig. 5b and c). However, as can be seen from the Raman results (Fig. 7c), it is evident that there is no continuous tribofilm on the surface and a significant increase in ID/IG ratio, indicating severe rupture of the graphene sheets. Hence, the decrease in the COF could be due to the shearing of more of the graphene layer bonded to the glycerol by hydrogen-bond interaction, as also proposed by Novikova et al. [6].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Building a tough and stable tribofilm
ce:section.ce:para: Raman mapping inside the worn surfaces of the specimens provided meaningful evidence for the superior lubrication of the GQDs in boundary lubrication (Fig. 7d). The variation in the ID/IG ratio in the Raman spectra reflected the degree of cracking of the graphene sheets [63]. The GQDs showed superior resistance to deformation and cracking due to a minimal change in ID/IG (Table 1), suggesting the formation of a tough tribo-film. At the same time, the appearance of a weak 2D band was attributed to the disruption in the c-axis of the graphite-like ordered structure [64], possibly due to the formation of additional hydroxyl groups (Figs. 7d, 8, and 9), which could be a possible reason for their excellent ability to resist cracking [63,65]. Moreover, the Raman mapping revealed that the tribofilm formed by the GQDs had more surface coverage than the surface lubricated with the Graq, GOaq and GNPsaq (Fig. 7d). This is due to the larger physical interaction of the negatively charged GQDs with positively charged sites of the worn surface, owing to their more negative ζ potential of − 51.16 mV compared to the pristine graphene, see Fig. S14. In addition, the electron-donating ability of the nitrogen heteroatoms in the GQDs [31], as well as the presence of N-containing functional groups (in addition to O-containing groups) facilitate the rapid and easier adsorption of the GQDs onto the positively charged worn surface [66], as evidenced by greater surface coverage (Fig. 7d). This further improved the stability and compactness of the tribofilm and protected the surface from severe wear.

In contrast to the GQDsaq, Raman mapping on the surfaces lubricated with the Graq, GOaq, and GNPsaq showed no continuous transfer film and an increase in the ID/IG ratio (Table 1), implying that the graphene sheets cracked severely and became very disordered as a result of the mechanical rubbing. Moreover, the decrease in the intensity of the 2D band, the broadening, and the shifting of G band towards higher wavenumbers indicate a loss in crystallinity, which can be attributed to the sliding-induced disordering and the structural degradation of the graphite layer [67]. All these findings suggest that the graphene layers inside the Gr, GO, and GNPs have an inferior crack resistance [63,68]. Furthermore, as Georgakilas et al. [19] suggested, the ruptured GO nanoplatelets tend to aggregate through π − π stacking and form coarse graphite-oxide particles. Therefore, instead of forming a continuous film (Fig. 7a, b, and c), these coarse aggregates squeeze out from the contact rather than penetrating the nano-valleys (Fig. 7b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lubrication mechanism in mixed-lubrication regime for 2D graphitic materials
ce:section.ce:para: It is essential to determine the lubrication regime to understand the lubrication mechanism. As shown in Fig. 5e and Fig. S12, the Graq, GOaq, and GNPsaq provide super-low friction in a mixed-lubrication regime. At the same time, our findings suggest that the synergetic effect of these materials with aqueous glycerol is similar and hence they have a similar lubrication mechanism, as proposed in Fig. 10. The 2D Raman mapping of these materials indicates no continuous tribofilm on the surface. Moreover, the significant increase in the ID/IG ratio indicates the severe rupture of the graphene sheets during reciprocating motion (Fig. 7a, b, and c). This causes an increase in the dangling bonds at the newly created edges in a graphene monolayer [69]. These edges are passivated by the H and OH from the glycerol-water mixture (inset of Fig. 10) [69], indicating an increase in the amount of graphene bonded to glycerol by the hydrogen-bond interaction. It is reported that the degradation of glycerol to acidic species leads to an increase in the number of hydroxyl-terminated groups that interact with the surface via hydrogen-bond interaction, causing a gentle polishing of the contact surfaces [8]. The polishing effect causes an increase in the value of λ (see supplement material, Note S6). Thus, the shearing of the graphene layer (Fig. 10a), the formation of a hydrated graphitic-glycerol network (Fig. 10b), and the low friction at the glycerol-graphene interface (Fig. 10c) contribute to the super-low friction (Fig. 10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lubrication mechanism in boundary-lubrication regime for GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Another important finding is that the GQDs provide super-low friction in boundary lubrication under a high contact pressure of 206 MPa, which is much higher than the Graq, GOaq, and GNPsaq, where it was 112, 87, and 107 MPa, respectively. Therefore, a model for super-low friction was established, as shown in Fig. 11. The model consists of several simultaneous mechanisms, which jointly provide unique and excellent properties of GQDs compared to other carbon-based particles.
(1)
Usually, the shear sliding of the graphene layers is considered as a lubrication mechanism for 2D graphitic materials. However, the GQDs are zero-dimensional materials with multilayered graphene structures inside their dots, as suggested by Fig. 3a. They consist of one or more layers of graphene, which are functionalized graphene nano-fragments with chemical groups at the edges [60]. Therefore, when the contact surfaces slide against each other, their relative motion supplies the shear stress to the GQDs, causing the shearing of the graphene sheets inside the GQDs, which could be one of the possible lubrication mechanisms (Fig. 11b).
(2)
Secondly, as indicated in Fig. 4, the GQDs exhibit excellent dispersion stability in aqueous glycerol owing to their very small size, high edge-to-volume ratio, and large surface area. In addition, the presence of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the GQDs (Fig. 3e) further facilitates uniform dispersion. Accordingly, the uniform distribution within the fluid ensures their presence at the instance of asperity interactions and so the solid-lubricant film formation during the pressure and shear in these asperity contacts (Fig. 11a). This leads to a continuous supply of GQDs between the asperity contacts during sliding, providing increased surface coverage of the film compared to other studied materials, as evidenced in Fig. 7d. Therefore, having more of the GQDs film at the surface than in other graphitic solid lubricant materials (see Fig. 7), confirms their effectiveness. This is also consistent with the low wear and extreme dispersion stability, confirming the fundamental advantage of GQDs.
(3)
The third essential part of the lubrication mechanism is that the GQDs strongly adhere to the surface. It is essential that when solid-lubricant tribolfilm is formed in-situ at the surface due to the asperity interactions, this film also remains at the asperity interaction site, and not being removed due to asperity shear, which would otherwise expose the surfaces for wear and higher friction. This crucial property of GQDs is evidenced by Raman results at point 2 in Fig. 7d and illustrated in Fig. 11c. Furthermore, the more negative ζ potential (Fig. S14) suggest the formation of shear-resistant lubricating film via electrostatic attraction on the positively charged sites of the worn surface (Fig. 11d).
(4)
Furthermore, both GQDs and the asperities are covered with OH groups, as evidenced by abundant C-OH on the worn steel surface (Fig. 9), which prevents the direct interaction between the asperities due to repulsive interactions between OH-OH groups [70]. This causes the GQDs to slip past through the asperities, resulting in lower wear and friction (Fig. 11e), which is the fourth specific property of GQDs that result in superior behaviour.
(5)
The fifth suggested distinguishing feature of the GQDs boundary lubrication mechanism is that the in-situ tribofilm also withstands the normal load without breaking, which would otherwise allow direct interaction between the two asperities and so poorer tribological performance. The results in Table 1 indicate that the GQDs suffer from a minimal change in ID/IG ratio and therefore exhibit a higher deformation resistance than other studied materials. It is suggested that this is due to the appearance of a weak 2D band after friction (Fig. 7d), which is caused by the disruption of the c-axis of the graphite-like ordered structure [51,64]. This probably occurs due to the formation of additional hydroxyl groups as suggested by UV–vis and XPS results (Figs. 8 and 9), which could be a reason for their excellent ability to resist deformation and cracking [63,65].

By proposing the above five simultaneous key mechanisms for excellent performance of GQDs in this study, we would like to highlight another aspect; a possible distinction from previous suggestions on the superior properties of GQDs. Namely, several studies have reported a possible rolling effect of GQDs [5,11,21]. However, the layered solid lubricants must exfoliate and provide low-shear between these individual layers (basal planes), which is their fundamental low-friction mechanism that has been vastly demonstrated in the past and also in this work. The more disordered and the more amorphous materials are, the more superior lubrication by easy shear between its layers can be achieved [71]. Since we observed an adhered coherent layer of GQDs providing the excellent shearing effect, it is difficult to justify at the same time also the rolling effect. Namely, rolling implies the resistance to normal load and to exfoliation and maintaining the geometry and form of the particles that can roll, which contradicts with exfoliated graphene sheets. We provided the evidence that GQDs shear by exfoliation, and stick to the surface due to the strong electrostatic interaction, which is responsible for good coverage and strong anchoring to the surface that also support the primary mechanism of shearing the basal planes. In contrast, we could not find direct evidence of possible rolling, and by apparent contradiction to above described mechanisms, it is believed that rolling is less likely to be the essential part of excellent GQDs behavior in this work.

Therefore, we believe that the mechanism of boundary lubrication of GQDs is a combination of at least five simultaneous beneficial effects (Fig. 11) that are not observed in other graphitic materials, namely: (1) the exfoliation and shearing of graphene layers inside the GQDs, (2) increased surface coverage due to the continuous supply of GQDs owing to their superior dispersion stability, (3) the in-situ formation of an adherent tribofilm due to electrostatic interaction that ensures resistance to removal due to asperity shear, (4) the abundance of OH groups on asperities and GQDs prevents the asperity contact due to OH-OH repulsion, and (5) the formation of a strong and tough tribofilm contribute to maintaining the super-low friction under a high contact pressure in the boundary-lubrication regime.

This study compared the lubrication behaviors of a family of graphene materials with aqueous glycerol in a self-mated steel contact under reciprocating sliding motion. It was demonstrated that the addition of 0.1 wt% of GQDs to the aqueous glycerol improves the frictional (72% reduction) and anti-wear performance (53%) in the boundary-lubrication regime under a contact pressure of 206 MPa. In the case of the 2D graphitic materials, no continuous tribofilm was observed, and the super-low friction was achieved in the mixed-lubrication regime, predominantly due to the polishing effect by the hydrated graphitic-glycerol network. It was also found that the GQDs possess superior deformation and crack resistance compared with the 2D graphitic materials and contribute to the development of a tough tribofilm. This excellent tribological performance of the GQDs in boundary lubrication is due to the internal shearing of graphene layers inside the GQDs. In addition, the formation of a strongly adhered tough tribofilm with more surface coverage contributed to the realization of super-low friction under a high contact pressure. This study suggests that GQDs-based green nano-lubricants have great potential for sustainable engineering.
